headline: Shifting Grid Costs to Large Users
short_summary: This bill shifts the financial burden of grid infrastructure and capacity costs from residential ratepayers to large energy users, primarily data centers.
status: Last Action: Signed by President on 2026-04-22
supporters: State lawmakers (e.g., Del. Destiny LeVere Bolling, Sen. Louise Lucas), Dominion Energy (supports the legislation to lower costs for customers), residential customers
opponents: Data Center Coalition (initially concerned about language)
news_headline: Virginia bill aims to cut residential energy costs by shifting expenses to data centers
news_summary: A Virginia bill, HB 1393 (and SB 253), aims to shift energy costs for new distribution infrastructure and capacity auction prices onto high-load energy users like data centers, potentially saving residential customers around $5.50 per month. Governor Spanberger's amendments to the bill were accepted, which aim to save money for residential customers by restricting Dominion's spending on line undergrounding and capping its return on equity.

headline: Data Center Water Use Reporting Mandate
short_summary: This bill mandates that water providers disclose data center water consumption to the State Water Control Board, increasing transparency regarding water usage.
status: Last Action: Acts of Assembly Chapter text (CHAP0896) on 2026-04-13
supporters: Sen. Kannan Srinivasan, environmental groups (e.g., Piedmont Environmental Council, National Parks Conservation Association), residents concerned about water resources
opponents: Not explicitly stated, but industry has historically lacked transparency
news_headline: Potomac River Named Most Endangered in U.S. for 2026, Citing Data Center Growth and Water Demands
news_summary: The Potomac River was named most endangered in 2026 due to data center growth and water demands, highlighting insufficient transparency and lack of cumulative impact assessment regarding water usage. SB 553, which passed, requires utilities to report monthly water volumes supplied to data centers to the Department of Environmental Quality starting in 2027, though critics note it lacks details on individual data centers or peak usage.

headline: Data Center Siting and Noise Regulations
short_summary: This bill requires site assessments for new data centers, including noise studies and potential impacts on local resources and communities, giving localities more regulatory power.
status: Last Action: Incorporated by Counties, Cities and Towns (HB153-Thomas) (Voice Vote) on 2026-01-23
supporters: Del. Joshua Thomas, local residents concerned about noise and siting, environmental groups
opponents: Data Center Coalition (generally opposes increased regulation/roadblocks)
news_headline: As data center noise concerns grow, Loudoun Co. officials discuss possible mitigation efforts
news_summary: Loudoun County residents are experiencing debilitating noise from data centers, leading officials to explore legislative avenues to address specific types of noise. HB 153 requires site assessments for new high energy use facilities, including sound profiles on nearby residential units and schools, and allows localities to examine impacts on water, agriculture, and historic sites.
